skills.teks_description: Determine various types of discontinuities and explore limitations of graphing calculators
skills.prompt: This topic covers advanced limits for 12th grade Mathematics students (TEKS P.2(L)). Students should evaluate complex limits and understand the limitations of graphing technology. Advanced limit techniques: L'Hopital's rule for indeterminate forms (0/0 and âˆž/âˆž), squeeze theorem (bounding a function between two that converge to the same limit), limits involving trigonometric functions (lim sin(x)/x = 1 as xâ†’0), limits involving exponential functions. Types of indeterminate forms: 0/0, âˆž/âˆž, 0Â·âˆž, âˆž-âˆž, 0^0, 1^âˆž, âˆž^0. Epsilon-delta definition: formal definition of limit; for every Îµ > 0, there exists Î´ > 0 such that |f(x) - L| < Îµ whenever 0 < |x - c| < Î´. Continuity: f is continuous at c if lim(xâ†’c)f(x) = f(c); types of discontinuities revisited with rigorous definitions. Calculator limitations: may miss holes, vertical asymptotes can appear as lines, pixel limitations affect graph accuracy. Vocabulary: L'Hopital's rule, squeeze theorem, indeterminate form, epsilon-delta, continuity, piecewise, removable discontinuity, essential discontinuity, limit at infinity.

skills.teks_description: Determine and analyze key features of functions including relative maximum, minimum, and zeros
skills.prompt: This topic covers advanced derivatives for 12th grade Mathematics students (TEKS P.2(I)). Students should apply differentiation techniques to analyze functions comprehensively. Advanced techniques: implicit differentiation (differentiate both sides with respect to x, solve for dy/dx), logarithmic differentiation (take ln of both sides for complex products/quotients), derivatives of inverse trig functions, parametric derivatives (dy/dx = (dy/dt)/(dx/dt)). Advanced applications: curve sketching (first derivative test, second derivative test, concavity, inflection points), optimization (maximize/minimize real-world quantities), related rates (differentiate equations relating changing quantities with respect to time), linear approximation (tangent line approximation f(x) â‰ˆ f(a) + f'(a)(x-a)), Newton's method for finding roots. Mean Value Theorem: if f is continuous on [a,b] and differentiable on (a,b), there exists c where f'(c) = (f(b)-f(a))/(b-a). Vocabulary: implicit differentiation, logarithmic differentiation, related rates, optimization, concavity, inflection point, Mean Value Theorem, linear approximation, Newton's method, critical point.

skills.teks_description: Evaluate finite sums and geometric series when possible, written in sigma notation
skills.prompt: This topic covers advanced integration for 12th grade Mathematics students (TEKS P.5(A)). Students should master integration techniques and apply them to various problems. Techniques: u-substitution (reverse chain rule), integration by parts (âˆ«u dv = uv - âˆ«v du), partial fractions (decompose rational functions), trigonometric substitution, trigonometric integrals. Applications: area between curves A = âˆ«[a,b] (f(x)-g(x))dx, volume of revolution (disk method V = Ï€âˆ«rÂ²dx, washer method, shell method V = 2Ï€âˆ«xf(x)dx), arc length L = âˆ«âˆš(1+(dy/dx)Â²)dx, surface area of revolution, work W = âˆ«F(x)dx, center of mass, probability. Improper integrals: infinite limits of integration or discontinuous integrands; evaluate as limits; convergent or divergent. Numerical integration: trapezoidal rule, Simpson's rule for approximation. Vocabulary: u-substitution, integration by parts, partial fractions, disk method, washer method, shell method, arc length, improper integral, convergent, divergent, trapezoidal rule, antiderivative.

skills.teks_description: Generate and solve exponential equations in mathematical and real-world problems
skills.prompt: This topic covers introductory differential equations for 12th grade Mathematics students (TEKS P.5(I)). Students should solve basic differential equations and apply them to exponential models. A differential equation relates a function to its derivatives. Separable equations: dy/dx = f(x)g(y); separate variables and integrate both sides. First-order linear: dy/dx + P(x)y = Q(x); use integrating factor e^(âˆ«P dx). Exponential growth/decay: dy/dt = ky has solution y = y0e^(kt); k > 0 growth, k < 0 decay. Logistic growth: dP/dt = rP(1 - P/K); solution approaches carrying capacity K. Slope fields: visual representation of differential equation solutions; each point shows slope dy/dx; solution curves follow the field. Euler's method: numerical approximation; yn+1 = yn + hÂ·f(xn,yn). Applications: population dynamics, radioactive decay, Newton's law of cooling, mixing problems, circuit analysis (RC circuits). Vocabulary: differential equation, separable, initial condition, particular solution, general solution, slope field, Euler's method, exponential growth, logistic growth, integrating factor, order.

skills.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond Texas HS TEKS; builds on Precalculus P.2 and P.5 foundations
skills.prompt: This topic covers multivariable calculus for 12th grade Mathematics students (college-level extension beyond TEKS). Students should extend single-variable calculus concepts to functions of multiple variables. Functions of several variables: f(x,y) represents surfaces in 3D; domain is a region in the xy-plane; level curves (contour lines). Partial derivatives: âˆ‚f/âˆ‚x (differentiate with respect to x, treat y as constant); âˆ‚f/âˆ‚y similarly. Gradient: âˆ‡f = <âˆ‚f/âˆ‚x, âˆ‚f/âˆ‚y>; points in direction of maximum increase; perpendicular to level curves. Chain rule for partial derivatives. Optimization: critical points where âˆ‡f = 0; second derivative test using Hessian matrix determinant. Double integrals: âˆ«âˆ«f(x,y)dA; iterated integrals; represent volume under surface. Changing order of integration; polar coordinates for circular regions. Triple integrals for volume. Applications: mass, center of mass, moment of inertia, probability. Vocabulary: partial derivative, gradient, level curve, contour plot, double integral, iterated integral, Jacobian, polar coordinates, cylindrical coordinates, spherical coordinates, critical point, saddle point.

skills.skill: Vector calculus (vector fields, line integrals, surface integrals)
skills.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond Texas HS TEKS; builds on P.4(I) vectors and P.3(A) parametric equations
skills.prompt: This topic covers vector calculus for 12th grade Mathematics students (college-level extension). Students should understand vector fields and integration along curves and over surfaces. Vector fields: F(x,y) = <P(x,y), Q(x,y)>; assign a vector to each point in space; examples include gravitational, electric, and fluid velocity fields. Line integrals: âˆ«C FÂ·dr = âˆ« (P dx + Q dy); compute work done by force along a path. Path independence: F is conservative if line integral depends only on endpoints; equivalent to F = âˆ‡f for some scalar potential f; test: âˆ‚P/âˆ‚y = âˆ‚Q/âˆ‚x. Green's theorem: relates double integral over a region to line integral around its boundary; âˆ®(Pdx + Qdy) = âˆ«âˆ«(âˆ‚Q/âˆ‚x - âˆ‚P/âˆ‚y)dA. Surface integrals: integrate over surfaces in 3D; flux integrals âˆ«âˆ«FÂ·dS. Divergence theorem: relates flux through closed surface to triple integral of divergence. Stokes' theorem: relates line integral to surface integral of curl. Vocabulary: vector field, line integral, surface integral, conservative, potential function, curl, divergence, Green's theorem, Stokes' theorem, divergence theorem, flux, circulation.

skills.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond Texas HS TEKS; builds on 2A.3 systems and matrix operations
skills.prompt: This topic covers linear algebra for 12th grade Mathematics students (college-level extension). Students should understand vector spaces, linear transformations, and eigenvalues. Vector spaces: sets with vector addition and scalar multiplication satisfying axioms; examples include R^n, polynomial spaces, function spaces. Subspaces: subsets that are themselves vector spaces. Linear independence: vectors are independent if no vector is a linear combination of others. Basis: linearly independent spanning set; dimension = number of basis vectors. Linear transformations: T:Vâ†’W preserving addition and scalar multiplication; represented by matrices. Matrix operations: row reduction, rank (dimension of column space), nullity (dimension of null space), rank-nullity theorem. Eigenvalues and eigenvectors: Av = Î»v; found by solving det(A - Î»I) = 0 for Î», then (A - Î»I)v = 0 for v. Diagonalization: A = PDP^(-1) when n linearly independent eigenvectors exist. Applications: systems of differential equations, Markov chains, Google PageRank, computer graphics, quantum mechanics. Vocabulary: vector space, subspace, linear independence, basis, dimension, linear transformation, eigenvalue, eigenvector, determinant, rank, nullity, diagonalization, kernel, image.

skills.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond Texas HS TEKS; advanced abstract mathematics
skills.prompt: This topic covers introductory abstract algebra for 12th grade Mathematics students (college-level extension). Students explore algebraic structures that generalize familiar number systems. Groups: a set G with a binary operation satisfying closure, associativity, identity element, and inverse element. Examples: integers under addition, nonzero rationals under multiplication, symmetry groups (dihedral groups, symmetric groups), cyclic groups. Subgroups: subsets that form groups under the same operation; Lagrange's theorem (order of subgroup divides order of group). Group homomorphisms: structure-preserving maps between groups; isomorphisms are bijective homomorphisms. Rings: sets with two operations (addition forming abelian group, multiplication being associative with distributive property). Commutative rings, rings with unity, integral domains. Fields: commutative rings where every nonzero element has a multiplicative inverse; examples include Q, R, C, finite fields. Applications: cryptography (RSA uses number theory and group theory), coding theory, crystallography, physics. Vocabulary: group, ring, field, binary operation, identity, inverse, closure, associativity, commutativity, subgroup, homomorphism, isomorphism, cyclic group, abelian group.

skills.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond Texas HS TEKS; advanced number theory topics
skills.prompt: This topic covers number theory for 12th grade Mathematics students (college-level extension). Students explore properties of integers and their applications. Divisibility: a|b means b = ka for some integer k; division algorithm b = qa + r (0 â‰¤ r < a). GCD: greatest common divisor; Euclidean algorithm for efficient computation; Bezout's identity ax + by = gcd(a,b). Prime numbers: integers > 1 with no positive divisors other than 1 and themselves; Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic (unique prime factorization); infinitely many primes (Euclid's proof). Modular arithmetic: a â‰¡ b (mod n) means n|(a-b); arithmetic with remainders; useful for cryptography and computer science. Fermat's little theorem: a^p â‰¡ a (mod p) for prime p. Euler's theorem: a^Ï†(n) â‰¡ 1 (mod n) when gcd(a,n) = 1; Ï† is Euler's totient function. Chinese Remainder Theorem. Applications: RSA encryption (relies on difficulty of factoring large numbers), hash functions, error-detecting codes, digital signatures. Vocabulary: divisibility, prime, composite, GCD, LCM, Euclidean algorithm, modular arithmetic, congruence, Fermat's theorem, Euler's totient, Chinese Remainder Theorem, RSA.

skills.teks_description: Discrete Mathematics for Computer Science - TEKS Chapter 126 Technology Applications
skills.prompt: This topic covers discrete mathematics for 12th grade Mathematics students (TEKS Â§126.37). Students should understand graph theory and combinatorics for computer science applications. Graph theory: graphs consist of vertices and edges; types include directed/undirected, weighted/unweighted, connected/disconnected, cyclic/acyclic. Key concepts: degree of vertex, paths, cycles, Euler paths/circuits (traverse every edge once), Hamiltonian paths/circuits (visit every vertex once), trees (connected acyclic graphs), graph coloring, planar graphs. Algorithms: BFS, DFS, Dijkstra's shortest path, minimum spanning tree (Kruskal's, Prim's). Combinatorics: permutations (order matters, P(n,r) = n!/(n-r)!), combinations (order doesn't matter, C(n,r) = n!/(r!(n-r)!)), pigeonhole principle, inclusion-exclusion, binomial theorem. Recurrence relations: Fibonacci sequence, solving linear recurrences. Applications: network design, scheduling, circuit design, social networks, data structures, algorithm analysis, coding theory. Vocabulary: graph, vertex, edge, degree, path, cycle, tree, Euler, Hamiltonian, planar, permutation, combination, binomial coefficient, recurrence relation, algorithm.

skills.teks_description: Analyze situations modeled by functions to solve real-world problems
skills.prompt: This topic covers mathematical modeling for 12th grade Mathematics students (TEKS P.2(N)). Students should analyze real-world situations and create mathematical models to solve problems. Modeling process: identify the problem, make simplifying assumptions, formulate a mathematical model (equation, function, system), solve mathematically, interpret results in context, validate and refine the model. Model types: linear (constant rate of change), exponential (constant percentage change), logistic (bounded growth), polynomial (multiple turning points), periodic/trigonometric (cyclical phenomena). Key concepts: choosing appropriate model type based on data behavior, fitting models to data using technology (regression), evaluating model quality (RÂ², residual analysis), recognizing model limitations and domain restrictions, sensitivity analysis (how changes in parameters affect output). Real-world applications: population growth, epidemic modeling (SIR model), financial planning, physics simulations, climate models, optimization of resources. Vocabulary: model, assumption, parameter, variable, fit, residual, interpolation, extrapolation, validation, sensitivity analysis, simulation, deterministic, stochastic.

skills.teks_description: Calculate confidence intervals for population means with known standard deviation
skills.prompt: This topic covers calculus-based statistics for 12th grade Mathematics students (TEKS S.6(C)). Students should apply calculus to probability and construct confidence intervals. Continuous probability distributions: probability density function (pdf) f(x) where P(a â‰¤ X â‰¤ b) = âˆ«[a,b] f(x)dx; total area = 1. Expected value: E(X) = âˆ«xf(x)dx. Variance: Var(X) = E(XÂ²) - [E(X)]Â². Normal distribution: f(x) = (1/(Ïƒâˆš(2Ï€)))e^(-(x-Î¼)Â²/(2ÏƒÂ²)); 68-95-99.7 rule; z-scores standardize. Confidence intervals for population mean: xÌ„ Â± z*(Ïƒ/âˆšn) when Ïƒ known; xÌ„ Â± t*(s/âˆšn) when Ïƒ unknown. Hypothesis testing: null and alternative hypotheses, test statistic, p-value, significance level Î±, Type I and Type II errors. Chi-squared tests for categorical data. Central Limit Theorem: sampling distribution of xÌ„ approaches normal as n increases regardless of population shape. Vocabulary: probability density function, expected value, variance, normal distribution, confidence interval, hypothesis test, significance level, p-value, Type I error, Type II error, Central Limit Theorem, chi-squared.

skills.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond Texas HS TEKS; advanced complex number analysis
skills.prompt: This topic covers introductory complex analysis for 12th grade Mathematics students (college-level extension). Students should explore functions of complex variables and their properties. Complex functions: f(z) where z = x + iy; maps the complex plane to the complex plane. Elementary functions: exponential e^z = e^x(cos y + i sin y), trigonometric (defined via exponential), logarithmic (multi-valued: log z = ln|z| + i(arg z + 2Ï€n)). Analytic functions: complex differentiable; Cauchy-Riemann equations âˆ‚u/âˆ‚x = âˆ‚v/âˆ‚y and âˆ‚u/âˆ‚y = -âˆ‚v/âˆ‚x characterize analyticity. Contour integrals: âˆ®f(z)dz along paths in the complex plane. Cauchy's integral theorem: âˆ®f(z)dz = 0 for analytic functions on simply connected domains. Residue theorem: relates contour integrals to residues at singularities. Power series: Taylor and Laurent series for complex functions. Applications: fluid dynamics, electrostatics, signal processing, evaluating real integrals. Vocabulary: analytic function, Cauchy-Riemann equations, contour integral, residue, singularity, pole, branch cut, conformal mapping, Laurent series, holomorphic.

skills.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond Texas HS TEKS; rigorous foundations of calculus
skills.prompt: This topic covers introductory real analysis for 12th grade Mathematics students (college-level extension). Students should understand rigorous foundations of calculus. Completeness: the real numbers have no gaps (every Cauchy sequence converges, every bounded set has a supremum). Sequences: convergence (for every Îµ > 0, there exists N such that |an - L| < Îµ for n > N), Cauchy sequences, monotone convergence theorem, Bolzano-Weierstrass theorem. Series: convergence tests (comparison, ratio, root, integral, alternating series tests), absolute vs. conditional convergence. Continuity: epsilon-delta definition, intermediate value theorem, extreme value theorem on closed intervals. Differentiability: formal definition, differentiability implies continuity (but not vice versa), Mean Value Theorem with proof, Taylor's theorem with remainder. Integration: Riemann integral as limit of Riemann sums, integrability of continuous functions, Fundamental Theorem of Calculus (rigorous statement and proof). Key concepts: proof techniques (direct, contradiction, induction, contrapositive), logical rigor, mathematical maturity. Vocabulary: supremum, infimum, convergence, Cauchy sequence, completeness, epsilon-delta, uniform continuity, pointwise convergence, uniform convergence, compactness.

skills.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond Texas HS TEKS; advanced mathematical structures
skills.prompt: This topic covers introductory topology for 12th grade Mathematics students (college-level extension). Students should explore fundamental concepts of topological spaces and continuous deformations. Topology: study of properties preserved under continuous deformations (stretching, bending, but not tearing or gluing). Open and closed sets: open set contains a neighborhood around each point; closed set contains all its limit points; a set can be both, neither, open, or closed. Topological spaces: set X with a collection of open sets satisfying axioms (empty set and X are open, arbitrary unions of open sets are open, finite intersections of open sets are open). Metric spaces: topology induced by a distance function. Homeomorphism: continuous bijection with continuous inverse; topologically equivalent spaces (donut â‰ˆ coffee cup). Key properties: connectedness (cannot be split into two disjoint open sets), compactness (every open cover has a finite subcover), Hausdorff property. Euler characteristic: V - E + F for polyhedra; topological invariant. Fundamental group: algebraic structure capturing loops in a space. Applications: data analysis (topological data analysis), network theory, physics, robotics, computer graphics. Vocabulary: topology, open set, closed set, neighborhood, continuous, homeomorphism, connectedness, compactness, metric space, manifold, Euler characteristic, genus.

skills.teks_description: Explore limitations of graphing calculators and numerical methods for approximation
skills.prompt: This topic covers numerical analysis for 12th grade Mathematics students (TEKS P.2(L)). Students should understand computational methods for approximating mathematical solutions and the limitations of graphing technology. Root-finding: bisection method (reliable but slow), Newton's method (fast but may not converge), secant method. Interpolation: Lagrange interpolation, Newton's divided differences, spline interpolation for smooth curves. Numerical integration: rectangle rule, trapezoidal rule, Simpson's rule; error analysis for each method. Numerical differentiation: finite difference approximations (forward, backward, central differences). Solving differential equations: Euler's method (first-order), improved Euler (Heun's method), Runge-Kutta methods (higher accuracy). Error analysis: truncation error (from method approximation), round-off error (from finite precision), stability, convergence, and accuracy trade-offs. Calculator/computer limitations: floating-point representation, loss of significance in subtraction, overflow/underflow. Vocabulary: numerical method, approximation, convergence, stability, truncation error, round-off error, iteration, algorithm, interpolation, extrapolation, Newton's method, Euler's method, Simpson's rule.

skills.teks_description: Apply mathematics to problems arising in everyday life, society, and the workplace
skills.prompt: This topic covers the history of mathematics for 12th grade Mathematics students (TEKS A.1(A)). Students should understand how mathematical ideas developed and their impact on everyday life, society, and the workplace. Ancient mathematics: Babylonian (base-60 system, quadratic equations), Egyptian (practical geometry, fractions), Greek (axiomatic reasoning, Euclid's Elements, Pythagorean theorem, proof concept, Archimedes' contributions). Medieval contributions: Islamic mathematicians (algebra from al-Khwarizmi, decimal system transmission), Indian mathematicians (zero as a number, negative numbers, early calculus concepts by Madhava). Renaissance and early modern: Fibonacci (Hindu-Arabic numerals in Europe), development of symbolic algebra, Descartes (coordinate geometry), Fermat and Pascal (probability). Calculus: Newton and Leibniz independently developed calculus; controversy over priority. Modern mathematics: Gauss (number theory), Euler (analysis, graph theory), Riemann (geometry, integration), Cantor (set theory, infinity), Godel (incompleteness theorems), Turing (computation theory). Impact: mathematics drives technology, science, engineering, economics, cryptography, AI. Vocabulary: axiom, proof, algebra, geometry, calculus, number theory, set theory, computation, algorithm, mathematical notation.

skills.teks_description: Write exponential functions describing growth and decay including compound interest models
skills.prompt: This topic covers financial mathematics for 12th grade Mathematics students (TEKS A.9(C)). Students should apply exponential functions to financial models including compound interest. Simple interest: I = Prt, A = P(1 + rt). Compound interest: A = P(1 + r/n)^(nt) where P = principal, r = annual rate, n = compounding frequency, t = years. Continuous compounding: A = Pe^(rt). Effective annual rate: reff = (1 + r/n)^n - 1. Annuities: regular payments; future value FV = PMT Ã— ((1+r/n)^(nt) - 1)/(r/n); present value PV = PMT Ã— (1 - (1+r/n)^(-nt))/(r/n). Loan amortization: calculate monthly payment, create amortization schedule showing principal and interest portions. Key concepts: time value of money (dollar today worth more than dollar tomorrow), NPV (net present value) for investment decisions, APR vs. APY, Rule of 72 (doubling time â‰ˆ 72/rate%). Applications: savings plans, mortgage calculations, retirement planning, credit card debt, bond valuation, stock options basics. Vocabulary: principal, interest rate, compound interest, annuity, amortization, present value, future value, APR, APY, effective rate, time value of money.

skills.teks_description: Compare statistical measures from simulated sampling distributions to theoretical distributions
skills.prompt: This topic covers actuarial science for 12th grade Mathematics students (TEKS S.5(D)). Students should apply statistical distributions to risk assessment and compare simulated to theoretical distributions. Actuarial science: uses mathematics, statistics, and financial theory to study uncertain future events, especially in insurance and pension contexts. Key concepts: probability distributions for modeling risk (normal, binomial, Poisson, exponential), expected value as measure of average outcome, variance and standard deviation as measures of risk, law of large numbers (actual results converge to expected value with large samples). Life tables: mortality rates by age, life expectancy calculations, survival functions. Insurance principles: premium calculation (expected loss + expenses + profit margin), pooling of risk, diversification, moral hazard, adverse selection. Simulation: Monte Carlo methods generate random scenarios to estimate probabilities and outcomes; compare simulation results to theoretical distributions. Applications: life insurance pricing, pension fund management, health insurance, property/casualty insurance, financial risk management. Vocabulary: actuary, risk, premium, mortality rate, life table, expected value, variance, probability distribution, Monte Carlo simulation, law of large numbers, adverse selection, moral hazard.

skills.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond Texas HS TEKS; optimization and decision science
skills.prompt: This topic covers operations research for 12th grade Mathematics students (college-level extension). Students should apply mathematical optimization techniques to decision-making problems. Linear programming: maximize or minimize a linear objective function subject to linear inequality constraints; feasible region is a convex polygon; optimal solution at a vertex (corner point method). Simplex method: algebraic approach for larger problems. Integer programming: when variables must be whole numbers. Key concepts: formulating optimization problems, identifying constraints and objective functions, sensitivity analysis (how changes in parameters affect optimal solution). Network optimization: shortest path (Dijkstra's algorithm), minimum spanning tree, maximum flow, transportation and assignment problems. Queuing theory: modeling waiting lines; arrival rates, service rates, queue length, waiting time. Decision theory: decision trees, expected monetary value, utility theory, game theory (Nash equilibrium, zero-sum games). Scheduling: critical path method (CPM), PERT for project management. Applications: supply chain logistics, airline scheduling, resource allocation, inventory management, military planning. Vocabulary: optimization, linear programming, objective function, constraint, feasible region, simplex, integer programming, network, queue, decision tree, game theory, Nash equilibrium.

skills.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond Texas HS TEKS; builds on number theory and discrete math foundations
skills.prompt: This topic covers cryptography for 12th grade Mathematics students (college-level extension building on number theory and discrete math). Students should understand mathematical foundations of secure communication. Classical ciphers: Caesar cipher (shift), Vigenere cipher (polyalphabetic), substitution ciphers; vulnerable to frequency analysis. Symmetric encryption: same key for encryption and decryption; AES (Advanced Encryption Standard) widely used; fast but key distribution problem. Asymmetric (public-key) encryption: RSA algorithm uses difficulty of factoring large numbers; public key encrypts, private key decrypts; based on Euler's theorem and modular arithmetic. RSA process: choose large primes p, q; compute n = pq, Ï†(n) = (p-1)(q-1); choose e coprime to Ï†(n); compute d = e^(-1) mod Ï†(n); public key (n,e), private key (n,d). Diffie-Hellman key exchange: allows two parties to establish shared secret over insecure channel; based on discrete logarithm problem. Hash functions: one-way functions producing fixed-length output; used for digital signatures, password storage, data integrity (SHA-256). Quantum computing threat: Shor's algorithm could break RSA; post-quantum cryptography research. Vocabulary: encryption, decryption, cipher, plaintext, ciphertext, key, RSA, public key, private key, modular arithmetic, hash function, digital signature, Diffie-Hellman, quantum computing.
